question: Card 1 — One-sample Z-test for \(\mu\) (\(\sigma\) known)

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use Linguistic: "\(\sigma\) is known to be …". Normal data or large \(n\) (CLT). Hypotheses \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\) (or \(\leq,\geq\)) vs. alternative. Statistic \(Z=(\bar X-\mu_0)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)\) Null distribution \(N(0,1)\) Reject (two-sided) \(|Z|>z_{\alpha/2}\) Reject (right) \(Z>z_\alpha\) Reject (left) \(Z<-z_\alpha\) CI ↔ duality \(\bar x\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sigma/\sqrt n\); two-sided rejects iff \(\mu_0\notin\) CI. Worked example \(\mu_0=500\) mL, \(\sigma=4\) mL, \(n=25\), \(\bar x=498.6\). \(z=(498.6-500)/(4/5)=-1.75\). \(|{-1.75}|<z_{0.025}=1.96\). p-value \(=2(1-\Phi(1.75))\approx 0.080\). Fail to reject. Pitfall Using \(s\) when \(\sigma\) was reported — that's a T-test. For one-sided, comparing \(|z|\) to \(z_{\alpha/2}\) instead of signed \(z\) to \(z_\alpha\).

question: Card 2 — One-sample T-test for \(\mu\) (\(\sigma\) unknown)

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use "\(s=\)…", no \(\sigma\) given; normal or moderately large \(n\). Statistic \(T_{n-1}=(\bar X-\mu_0)/(S/\sqrt n)\) Null distribution \(t_{n-1}\) Reject (two-sided) \(|T|>t_{\alpha/2,n-1}\) CI \(\bar x\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-1}\,s/\sqrt n\) Worked example (slide 19.2, p. 438) Fiber breaking strength, \(n=15\), \(\bar x=152.18\) psi, \(s^2=16.63\). \(t=(152.18-150)/\sqrt{16.63/15}=2.07\); \(t_{0.025,14}=2.145\); fail to reject \(H_0:\mu=150\). Pitfall Forgetting d.f. \(=n-1\); using \(z\) instead of \(t\) for small \(n\); plugging \(s^2\) where \(s\) is needed. Demo D2 — t vs. z distribution comparator (Ch 20 prereq, used by every T-card) d.f. = 5 Slide d.f. up: the heavy-tailed t shrinks toward the standard normal (dashed). At d.f.\(\geq 30\), the two-sided 95% critical values agree to ≈ 0.02. Use t when \(\sigma\) was estimated from the data.

question: Card 3 — \(\chi^2\)-test for one variance

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use Inference on \(\sigma\) or \(\sigma^2\) from a single normal sample. Assumption Normal data — required, no CLT escape [slide 444] . Statistic \(\chi^2_{n-1}=(n-1)S^2/\sigma_0^2\) Reject (two-sided) \(\chi^2>\chi^2_{\alpha/2,n-1}\) or \(\chi^2<\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,n-1}\) Reject (right) \(\chi^2>\chi^2_{\alpha,n-1}\) Reject (left) \(\chi^2<\chi^2_{1-\alpha,n-1}\) CI for \(\sigma^2\) \([(n-1)s^2/\chi^2_{\alpha/2,n-1},\,(n-1)s^2/\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,n-1}]\) Worked example (slide 19.5, p. 445–446) \(n=20\), \(s=1.41\) mm, claim \(\sigma<1.5\). \(H_0:\sigma\geq 1.5\), one-sided left . \(\chi^2_{19}=19\cdot 1.41^2/2.25=16.79\); critical \(\chi^2_{0.95,19}=10.117\); \(16.79>10.117\). Fail to reject. Erratum (slide 446) The slide's wording is correct but easily misread: the test is left-tailed, so reject when \(\chi^2 < \chi^2_{1-\alpha,n-1}\). Some students mis-parse this as right-tailed and would (incorrectly) reject. The arithmetic on the slide is fine. Pitfall Treating the test as CLT-robust — it isn't. Confusing \(\sigma\) with \(\sigma^2\) in \(H_0\). Reading the wrong tail of the table — remember \(\chi^2_{0.95,\nu}<\chi^2_{0.05,\nu}\). Demo D3 — chi-squared shape with critical regions (Ch 19) d.f. = 19 \(\alpha\) = 0.05 tail two-sided right (variance ↑) left (variance ↓) Note the skew: even at d.f.=30 the chi-square is still right-skewed. The lower critical value is much closer to 0 than the upper is far from the mean. Don't mentally symmetrise.

topic_tags: Card 4 — Sign test for the median
question: Card 4 — Sign test for the median

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use Inference on the median , no distributional assumption beyond continuity. Statistic \(Q^+=\#\{X_i>M_0\}\), \(Q^-=\#\{X_i<M_0\}\); drop ties at \(M_0\). Null distribution \(Q^+\sim\mathrm{Bin}(n,1/2)\) Reject (right, \(H_0:M\leq M_0\)) if \(P[Q^-\leq q^-_{\rm obs}]<\alpha\) Reject (left, \(H_0:M\geq M_0\)) if \(P[Q^+\leq q^+_{\rm obs}]<\alpha\) Reject (two-sided) \(P[\min(Q^+,Q^-)\leq k]<\alpha/2\) Worked example (slide 20.3, p. 457–458) Six-sided die, \(n=20\). \(H_0:M\leq 3.5\). \(Q^-=6\). \(P[Q^-\leq 6\mid M=3.5]=\sum_{x=0}^6\binom{20}{x}/2^{20}=0.0577\). Fail to reject (borderline). Pitfall Computing \(\bar X\) — this is a sign test, not a mean test. Forgetting to drop ties at \(M_0\). Using \(Q^+\) for \(H_0:M\leq M_0\) — must be \(Q^-\) [slide 456] .

question: Card 5 — Wilcoxon signed-rank test

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use Median inference, willing to assume symmetry but not normality. Procedure Rank \(|X_i-M_0|\) smallest→largest; assign signs from \(X_i-M_0\); \(W^+=\)sum of positive ranks; \(W^+ + W^- = n(n+1)/2\). Statistic (small \(n\)) \(W=\min(W^+,W^-)\); compare with Wilcoxon table. Statistic (large \(n\geq 20\)) \(W^+\approx N(n(n+1)/4,\,n(n+1)(2n+1)/24)\); standardise to \(Z\). With ties, subtract \(\sum(t^3-t)/48\) from variance. Erratum (slide 467) The displayed normal-approximation arithmetic on slide 467 is mangled by PDF extraction. The numerical answer \(z=-1.977\) is correct: with ties \(t=\{10,5,5\}\) the variance correction is \(717.5 - (990+120+120)/48\approx 691.9\), so \(z=(53-105)/\sqrt{691.9}=-1.977\). Reproduce by hand if the slide's intermediate steps look garbled. Worked example pH measurements: 7.05, 7.10, 6.95, 7.20, 6.85, 7.15. \(M_0=7.00\). Differences: \(+0.05,+0.10,-0.05,+0.20,-0.15,+0.15\). Absolute ranks (averaging the tied 0.05's): 1.5, 3, 1.5, 6, 4.5, 4.5. \(W^+=1.5+3+6+4.5=15\); \(W^-=1.5+4.5=6\). Two-sided \(\alpha=0.05\), \(n=6\): table critical ≈ 0; \(\min(15,6)=6\); fail to reject. Demo D8 — sign test vs. Wilcoxon SR on the same data (Ch 20) Data (comma-separated) \(M_0\) Wilcoxon uses the magnitudes (ranks of \(|X-M_0|\)); the sign test discards them. When the signal is large but distributed across many small magnitudes, Wilcoxon detects it; sign test misses. Cost: Wilcoxon needs symmetry.

question: Card 6 — One-sample proportion Z-test

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use "\(x\) out of \(n\)"; large enough that \(np_0\geq 5\) AND \(n(1-p_0)\geq 5\). Statistic (test) \(Z = (\hat p-p_0)/\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}\) CI \(\hat p\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}\) [slide 478] Sample size \(n=z_{\alpha/2}^2\hat p(1-\hat p)/d^2\); conservative \(n=z_{\alpha/2}^2/(4d^2)\) [slide 480] Erratum (slides 478/482) — the famous denominator mismatch The test denominator uses \(\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}\) (the SE under \(H_0\)) [slide 482] . The CI denominator uses \(\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}\) (since under a CI we don't have a \(p_0\)) [slide 478] . Mixing them is the single most common exam error in Ch 21. The slides show both formulas only four pages apart and offer no explicit reconciliation, so reading hastily often produces a hybrid that's wrong. Worked example (slide 21.1, p. 479) \(n=2079\), \(\hat p=0.485\). 99% CI: \(0.485\pm 2.576\sqrt{0.485\cdot 0.515/2079}=0.485\pm 0.028\). For a test of \(H_0:p=0.5\) we'd use \(\sqrt{0.5\cdot 0.5/2079}\) instead.

question: Card 8 — F-test for two variances

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content:
Assumption Both populations normal ; independent samples. No CLT escape. Statistic \(F=S_1^2/S_2^2\) under \(H_0:\sigma_1=\sigma_2\); \(\sim F_{n_1-1,n_2-1}\). Reject (two-sided) \(S_1^2/S_2^2>f_{\alpha/2,n_1-1,n_2-1}\) or \(S_2^2/S_1^2>f_{\alpha/2,n_2-1,n_1-1}\). Reciprocal trick \(f_{1-\alpha,
u_1,
u_2}=1/f_{\alpha,
u_2,
u_1}\) [slide 496] Pre-test caveat F-test is often performed at \(\alpha=0.10\) or \(0.20\) when used as a gateway (failing to reject is the desired outcome — small \( eta\) matters more than small \(\alpha\)) [slide 499] . The course also flags that pre-testing for variance equality on the same data and then choosing pooled vs. Welch invalidates the p-value [slides 521–522] . Modern recommendation: always Welch [slide 527] . Worked example (slide 22.7, p. 500) \(n_1=n_2=8\), \(s_1^2=4.02\), \(s_2^2=3.89\). Two-sided \(\alpha=0.20\), \(\alpha/2=0.10\). \(F=1.03\); \(f_{0.10,7,7}=2.785\); fail to reject. Demo D4 — F-distribution shape (Ch 22) \(
u_1\) = 7 \(
u_2\) = 7 \(\alpha\) = 0.05 When \(
u_1<
u_2\) the F is heavily right-skewed; both d.f. \(	o\infty\) makes it concentrate at 1. The reciprocal trick reads off the lower critical from the upper of the swapped distribution.

question: Card 9 — Two-sample Z-test (\(\sigma_1,\sigma_2\) known)

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use Both \(\sigma_i\) given as fixed numbers; not common in practice but appears on exams. Statistic \(Z = \dfrac{(\bar X_1-\bar X_2)-(\mu_1-\mu_2)_0}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1+\sigma_2^2/n_2}}\sim N(0,1)\) CI \((\bar x_1-\bar x_2)\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1+\sigma_2^2/n_2}\) [slide 511] Worked example (slide 23.1, p. 507–511) Two production lines, \(\sigma_1^2=40,\sigma_2^2=50\), \(n_1=n_2=10\), \(\bar x_1=824.9, \bar x_2=818.6\). Test \(H_0:\mu_1\leq\mu_2\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1>\mu_2+10\), \(\alpha=0.05\). \(z=(824.9-818.6-10)/\sqrt{4+5}=-3.7/3=-1.23\) for the \(\mu_1-\mu_2-10\) version, or with the simpler null \(z=6.3/3=2.10>z_{0.05}=1.645\) ⇒ reject (depending on how you parse the alternative). Slide reads the simpler form and rejects.

question: Card 10 — Pooled (Student) T-test

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Assumption Both normal; equal variances ; independent. Pooled variance \(S_p^2 = \dfrac{(n_1-1)S_1^2+(n_2-1)S_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}\) Statistic \(T_{n_1+n_2-2}=\dfrac{(\bar X_1-\bar X_2)-(\mu_1-\mu_2)_0}{S_p\sqrt{1/n_1+1/n_2}}\) CI \((\bar x_1-\bar x_2)\pm t_{\alpha/2,n_1+n_2-2}\,s_p\sqrt{1/n_1+1/n_2}\) Worked example (slide 23.4, p. 514–515) \(n_1=12\), \(\bar x_1=24.6\), \(s_1=0.85\); \(n_2=15\), \(\bar x_2=22.1\), \(s_2=0.98\). \(s_p^2=(11\cdot 0.7225+14\cdot 0.9604)/25=0.8557\), \(s_p=0.925\). 95% CI: \(2.5\pm 2.060\cdot 0.925\sqrt{1/12+1/15}=2.5\pm 0.74\). Excludes 0 ⇒ reject equality of means at 5%.

question: Card 11 — Welch / Satterthwaite T-test

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content:
Assumption Both normal; no equal-variance assumption; independent. Statistic \(T_
u=\dfrac{( ar X_1- ar X_2)-(\mu_1-\mu_2)_0}{\sqrt{S_1^2/n_1+S_2^2/n_2}}\) d.f. (Welch–Satt.) \(
u = \dfrac{(s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2)^2}{(s_1^2/n_1)^2/(n_1-1)+(s_2^2/n_2)^2/(n_2-1)}\), round down [slide 527] Erratum (slide 527) The slide states "round down" but some print editions and student notes show \(
u=12.0\) where the correct rounded-down value is \(
u=11\) for the worked numbers. Always recompute and floor; never round to nearest. Worked example (same data as Card 10) \(s_1^2/n_1=0.0602\), \(s_2^2/n_2=0.0640\), sum 0.1242. \(
u=0.01543/(0.000330+0.000293)=24.78	o
u=24\). \(T=2.5/\sqrt{0.1242}=7.09\); \(t_{0.025,24}=2.064\); reject. Welch agrees with pooled here because \(s_1\approx s_2\). Demo D5 — Pooled vs. Welch comparator (Ch 23) \(n_1\) \(n_2\) \(s_1\) \(s_2\) \( ar x_1- ar x_2\) \(\alpha\) Make \(s_1\) and \(s_2\) very different (e.g. 0.5 and 3.0) and watch the Welch d.f. crash — that's exactly when pooled becomes wrong and Welch saves you. With equal \(s\)'s, pooled and Welch agree.

question: Card 12 — Paired T-test

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use Pairs \((X_i,Y_i)\): matched subjects, before/after, etc. Define \(D_i=X_i-Y_i\); test on \(\mu_D\). Statistic \(T_{n-1}=\bar D/(S_D/\sqrt n)\), \(n\) = number of pairs . Why pairing matters [slides 549–555] \(\sigma_D^2=(2\sigma^2/n)(1-\rho_{XY})\). If \(\rho_{XY}>0\), paired \(T\) is more powerful than pooled even with fewer d.f.; if \(\rho_{XY}\leq 0\), pairing loses power. Worked example (slide 24.4, p. 545–546) Weight before/after, \(n=16\) pairs, \(\bar d=-4.125\), \(s_D^2=16.517\). \(H_0:\mu_D\geq 0\). \(t=-4.125/\sqrt{16.517/16}=-4.06\); \(t_{0.01,15}=2.602\); \(-4.06<-2.602\); reject at \(\alpha=0.01\).

question: Card 13 — Wilcoxon rank-sum / Mann–Whitney U

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: When to use Two independent samples, non-parametric comparison of locations. Procedure Pool both, rank smallest→largest; \(W_m\) = sum of ranks for the smaller sample. Hypothesis \(H_0:P[X>Y]=1/2\); equivalent to equal medians if same shape. Large-sample \(Z\) \(E[W_m]=m(m+n+1)/2\), \(\mathrm{Var}=mn(m+n+1)/12\); tie correction subtracts \(\sum(t^3-t)/12\) from variance. Worked example (slide 24.2, p. 534–539) Vv557 midterm: \(m=14\) undergrads, \(n=30\) grads. Sum of undergrad ranks \(w_{14}=380\). \(E=315\), Var=1575, SD≈39.7. \(z=(380-315)/39.7=1.64\); one-sided p ≈ 0.0505. Marginal evidence undergrads do better.

question: Card 7 — Two-sample proportion Z-test

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: General (any \((p_1-p_2)_0\)) \(Z=\dfrac{\hat p_1-\hat p_2-(p_1-p_2)_0}{\sqrt{\hat p_1(1-\hat p_1)/n_1+\hat p_2(1-\hat p_2)/n_2}}\) [slide 485] Pooled (only when \(p_{10}=p_{20}\)) \(\hat p=(n_1\hat p_1+n_2\hat p_2)/(n_1+n_2)\); \(Z=\dfrac{\hat p_1-\hat p_2}{\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)(1/n_1+1/n_2)}}\) [slide 487] CI for \(p_1-p_2\) Always uses unpooled SE. CI vs. test denominators (the famous distinction) For testing \(H_0:p_1=p_2\), the natural variance reference under \(H_0\) is the common \(p\) — estimate it by pooling. For the CI, no \(H_0\) holds, so use \(\hat p_1\) and \(\hat p_2\) separately. Mixing the two is wrong ; this trips up most students. Worked example (slide 21.6, p. 488–489) Monday: 8/100. Other: 12/200. Pooled \(\hat p=20/300=0.0667\). \(z=0.02/\sqrt{0.0667\cdot 0.9333\cdot(1/100+1/200)}=0.658\). Right-tail p ≈ 0.255. Fail to reject.

question: Card 14 — Inferences on correlation \(\rho\)

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Sample \((X_i,Y_i)\) from a bivariate normal ; sample correlation \[ r = \frac{\sum(X_i-\bar X)(Y_i-\bar Y)}{\sqrt{\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2}\sqrt{\sum(Y_i-\bar Y)^2}}. \] T-test for \(\rho=0\) \(T_{n-2}=r\sqrt{n-2}/\sqrt{1-r^2}\sim t_{n-2}\) [slide 657] — exact only for \(\rho_0=0\). Fisher \(z\)-test for general \(\rho_0\) \(Z=\sqrt{n-3}(\operatorname{arctanh}(r)-\operatorname{arctanh}(\rho_0))\approx N(0,1)\) [slide 558] CI for \(\rho\) \(\tanh(\operatorname{arctanh}(r)\pm z_{\alpha/2}/\sqrt{n-3})\) [slide 559] Worked example \(n=20\), \(r=0.5\). Test \(\rho=0\): \(T=0.5\sqrt{18}/\sqrt{0.75}=2.45\); \(t_{0.025,18}=2.101\); reject. 95% CI: \(\operatorname{arctanh}(0.5)=0.5493\); \(z_{0.025}/\sqrt{17}=0.4754\); z-CI = [0.0739, 1.0247]; invert with \(\tanh\): \([0.074, 0.772]\). Pitfall Using the T-test formula for \(\rho_0\neq 0\). It is exact only at \(\rho_0=0\); for any other null use Fisher's \(z\). Forgetting to invert the CI with \(\tanh\) at the end.

question: Card 15 — Pearson \(\chi^2\) goodness-of-fit

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Setup Single sample of \(n\) observations grouped into \(k\) categories. Statistic \(X^2 = \sum_{i=1}^k (O_i-E_i)^2/E_i\), \(E_i = np_{i0}\). Null distribution \(\chi^2_{k-1-m}\) where \(m = \#\) parameters estimated from the data. Reject Always upper-tail: \(X^2 > \chi^2_{\alpha,k-1-m}\) [slide 573] . Cochran's rule \(E_i\geq 1\) for all \(i\); \(E_i\geq 5\) for at least 80% of cells. Pool low-\(E\) cells [slides 572, 581] . Erratum (slide 577) — d.f. for MoM fits The slides apply the d.f. correction \(k-1-m\) regardless of how the \(m\) parameters were estimated. Strictly, this result (Fisher's correction) is provable only for MLE-style estimates; for method-of-moments fits the limiting distribution is bounded above by \(\chi^2_{k-1-m}\) but not necessarily equal to it. The course treats both as identical; the exam will reward you for using \(k-1-m\) but you should be aware the slide is glossing over a subtlety. Worked example (slides 579–582, Poisson defects) \(n=60\) printed boards: counts 32, 15, 9, 4 in defect categories \(0,1,2,\geq 3\). Estimate \(\hat\lambda=\bar X = 0.75\) (\(m=1\)). Expected: \(28.32, 21.24, 7.98, 2.46\). Pool last two (Cochran): now \(k=3\) cells, expected \(28.32, 21.24, 10.44\). \(X^2=0.478+1.834+0.628=2.94\). d.f.\(=k-1-m=3-1-1=1\). \(\chi^2_{0.05,1}=3.84\); fail to reject.

question: Card 16 — \(\chi^2\) test of independence (\(r\times c\))

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Design One sample of \(n\); each subject cross-classified by two categorical variables. Hypothesis \(H_0: p_{ij} = p_{i\cdot}\,p_{\cdot j}\) for all \((i,j)\). Expected \(E_{ij}=n_{i\cdot}n_{\cdot j}/n\) Statistic \(X^2 = \sum_{i,j}(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2/E_{ij}\sim\chi^2_{(r-1)(c-1)}\)

question: Card 17 — \(\chi^2\) test of homogeneity

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Same arithmetic as Card 16, different design: \(r\) separate samples of fixed sizes \(n_{i\cdot}\). Test whether column proportions are the same across rows. \(H_0: p_{1j}=\dots=p_{rj}\) for each \(j\). The grader rewards you for naming the design correctly. Pitfall Wrong d.f. — always \((r-1)(c-1)\) regardless of how many parameters you "feel" were estimated. Pooling rows/cols to satisfy Cochran but forgetting to recompute d.f. from the new \(r,c\). Calling Card 17 a test of "independence" when row totals are fixed. Demo D9 — chi-squared on a 2×3 contingency table (Ch 25) C1 C2 C3 R1 R2 Edit any cell. Expected counts and \(X^2\) update live; d.f.\(=(r-1)(c-1)=2\). When all cells exactly match independence, \(X^2 = 0\). Cochran flag if any \(E_{ij}<5\).

topic_tags: Card 18 — Simple linear regression: fitting and inference
question: Card 18 — Simple linear regression: fitting and inference

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: L-I-N-H assumptions [slide 625] : L inearity \(\mu_{Y\mid x}=\beta_0+\beta_1 x\); I ndependence of observations; N ormality of \(Y\mid x\); H omoscedasticity (variance \(\sigma^2\) does not depend on \(x\)). Estimators [slide 619] : \[ S_{xx}=\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2,\;S_{xy}=\sum(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y),\;S_{yy}=\sum(y_i-\bar y)^2=\mathrm{SST} \] \[ b_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx},\quad b_0 = \bar y-b_1\bar x,\quad \mathrm{SSE} = S_{yy}-b_1 S_{xy},\quad S^2=\mathrm{SSE}/(n-2) \] Distributions [slides 626, 631] : \(B_1\sim N(\beta_1,\sigma^2/S_{xx})\); \(B_0\sim N(\beta_0,\sigma^2\sum x_i^2/(nS_{xx}))\); \((n-2)S^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n-2}\), independent of \((B_0,B_1)\). Slope T-test (significance of regression) \(T_{n-2}=B_1/(S/\sqrt{S_{xx}})\); reject \(H_0:\beta_1=0\) if \(|T|>t_{\alpha/2,n-2}\) [slide 635] Equivalent F-test \(F_{1,n-2}=(n-2)R^2/(1-R^2)=\mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE}\); \(T^2=F\) [slide 654] CI for \(\beta_1\) \(b_1\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-2}\,s/\sqrt{S_{xx}}\) CI for \(\mu_{Y\mid x^*}\) \(\hat\mu \pm t_{\alpha/2,n-2}\,s\sqrt{1/n + (x^*-\bar x)^2/S_{xx}}\) [slide 640] PI for new \(Y\mid x^*\) Same with \(1+\) added inside the radical [slide 645] Worked example (slide 26.1, p. 620–637 — solvent evaporation) \(n=25\), \(S_{xx}=7245.47\), \(S_{xy}=-575.78\), \(S_{yy}=63.89\). \(b_1=-0.0795\), \(b_0=13.60\). \(\mathrm{SSE}=18.13\), \(s^2=0.789\), \(s=0.888\). \(T_{23}=-0.0795/(0.888/\sqrt{7245.47})=-7.62\); reject \(\beta_1=0\) at any reasonable \(\alpha\). \(R^2=0.716\). 95% CI for \(\beta_1\): \(-0.0795\pm 0.0216\).

question: Card 19 — SLR prediction & diagnosis

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: CI vs. PI — the famous "+1" distinction. \[ \text{CI for }\mu_{Y\mid x^*}: \hat\mu\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-2}\,s\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{n}+\tfrac{(x^*-\bar x)^2}{S_{xx}}} \] \[ \text{PI for new }Y\mid x^*: \hat\mu\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-2}\,s\sqrt{1+\tfrac{1}{n}+\tfrac{(x^*-\bar x)^2}{S_{xx}}} \] The PI is always wider; the extra "1+" accounts for the noise of one new observation around its mean. Both bands flare at the ends — never extrapolate [slide 668] . Lack-of-fit F-test [slide 664] . Requires replicates . Let \(k\) = number of distinct \(x\)'s, \(n=\sum n_i\): \[ \mathrm{SSE}=\mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{pe}+\mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{lf},\quad \mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{pe}=\sum_i\sum_j(Y_{ij}-\bar Y_i)^2 \] \[ F_{k-2,n-k}=\frac{\mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{lf}/(k-2)}{\mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{pe}/(n-k)} \] Reject \(H_0\) "linear model is appropriate" if \(F>f_{\alpha,k-2,n-k}\). Residual diagnostics [slide 668] . Plot \(e_i\) vs. \(\hat y_i\) (look for horizontal band centred at 0; funnel = heteroscedasticity); \(e_i\) vs. \(x_i\) (curve = non-linearity); QQ-plot of \(e_i\) for normality. The Anscombe quartet [slide 671] shows four datasets with identical regression statistics but only one truly linear — plot first . Out-of-syllabus Cook's distance, leverage \(h_{ii}\), DFFITS, externally studentised residuals are mentioned at slide depth only . The course doesn't teach VIF for multicollinearity. scale-location plot , Cook's D thresholds , DFBETAS — flag as out-of-syllabus on the exam. Demo D6 — SLR fit + 95% CI band + 95% PI band (Ch 26–27) true \(\beta_0\) true \(\beta_1\) noise \(\sigma\) \(n\) = 20 Resample Inner blue band = 95% CI for \(\mu_{Y\mid x}\). Outer red band = 95% PI for a new \(Y\mid x\). Increase \(n\): the CI band shrinks toward the line; the PI band shrinks only modestly because it's dominated by the irreducible noise \(\sigma\). Increase \(\sigma\): both bands widen, PI more.

question: Card 20 — Multiple linear regression: estimation

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Model. \(Y = X\boldsymbol\beta + \mathbf E\), where \(X\) is the \(n\times(p+1)\) design matrix (column of 1's followed by \(p\) predictor columns) and \(\mathbf E\sim N(\mathbf 0,\sigma^2 I_n)\). Estimator [slide 681] : \(\mathbf b = (X^T X)^{-1}X^T Y\). Hat matrix \(H = X(X^TX)^{-1}X^T\) projects \(Y\) onto the column space; \(\hat Y = HY\). With \(P = \frac{1}{n}\mathbf 1\mathbf 1^T\): \[ \mathrm{SST}=Y^T(I-P)Y,\;\mathrm{SSE}=Y^T(I-H)Y,\;\mathrm{SSR}=Y^T(H-P)Y \] \(S^2 = \mathrm{SSE}/(n-p-1)\) is unbiased for \(\sigma^2\); \(R^2=\mathrm{SSR}/\mathrm{SST}\). Polynomial vs. multilinear use the same machinery: just put \(x_i^j\) in column \(j\) (polynomial) or the predictor values (multilinear). Indicator (dummy) variables [slides 744–753] : for an \(\ell\)-level qualitative variable, use \(\ell-1\) indicator columns. Add \(X_1 X_2\) interaction column to allow slopes (not just intercepts) to differ across groups.

topic_tags: Card 21 — MLR inference: three F's and a T
question: Card 21 — MLR inference: three F's and a T

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Distribution. \(\mathbf b\sim N(\boldsymbol\beta,\sigma^2(X^TX)^{-1})\) [slide 721] ; \(c_{jj}=[(X^TX)^{-1}]_{jj}\). Overall F (significance) \(H_0:\beta_1=\dots=\beta_p=0\); \(F_{p,n-p-1}=\mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE}=\frac{n-p-1}{p}\cdot\frac{R^2}{1-R^2}\) [slide 714] T-test on \(\beta_j\) \(T_{n-p-1}=b_j/(S\sqrt{c_{jj}})\); reject \(H_0:\beta_j=0\) if \(|T|>t_{\alpha/2,n-p-1}\) [slide 730] Partial F (extra-SS) full \(p\) preds vs. reduced \(m<p\) preds: \(F_{p-m,n-p-1}=\frac{n-p-1}{p-m}\cdot\frac{\mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{red}-\mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{full}}{\mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{full}}\) [slide 736] CI for \(\beta_j\) \(b_j\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-p-1}\,s\sqrt{c_{jj}}\) [slide 722] CI for \(\mu_{Y\mid \mathbf x_0}\) \(\hat\mu\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-p-1}\,s\sqrt{\mathbf x_0^T(X^TX)^{-1}\mathbf x_0}\) [slide 728] PI for new \(Y\mid \mathbf x_0\) add "1+" inside the radical [slide 729] Equivalences worth memorising The T-test on \(\beta_j=0\) is identical (in p-value and decision) to the partial F-test where the reduced model omits only \(X_j\) [slide 740] . Setting reduced = intercept-only and full = full model recovers exactly the overall F [slide 741] . Erratum (slide 715, F-statistic arithmetic) Slide 715's worked example reports \(F=243.05\) but the indicated \(R^2=0.9866\) and \((n,p)=(10,2)\) yield \(F = (7/2)\cdot 0.9866/0.0134 = 257.6\). The discrepancy is rounding in the slide's intermediate \(R^2\); recompute from the SSE and SSR if you need 3-digit accuracy. Worked example (slide 29.15, p. 737 — quadratic vs linear) \(n=7\) points. Full quadratic SSE = 1.219; reduced linear SSE = 2.531. Partial F (\(p=2,m=1\)): \(F_{1,4}=(4/1)\cdot(2.531-1.219)/1.219=4.31\); \(f_{0.05,1,4}=7.71\); fail to reject. No evidence the quadratic term is needed.

question: Card 22 — Model selection ()

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Three classical algorithms. Forward selection. Start empty. Add the predictor with the largest \(R^2\) increase whose addition is significant (partial F). Stop when none is. Backward elimination. Start full. Drop the predictor whose removal least decreases \(R^2\) and whose removal is not rejected by partial F. Stop when every remaining predictor is significant. Stepwise. Forward + backward check at each step. PRESS [slide 770] : \(\mathrm{PRESS}=\sum(y_i-\hat y_{i,(-i)})^2\) (leave-one-out). Smaller = better out-of-sample prediction. Course's warning [slides 767–768] These algorithms are known to be flawed : the multiplicity of tests inflates effective \(\alpha\); tests on the same data are not independent; resulting \(R^2\) is over-optimistic; post-selection CIs are too narrow. The slide says "Never do this!" — but the course still expects you to execute the procedure. What the slides do NOT cover Adjusted \(R^2\) , AIC/BIC , VIF . Slide 772 mentions "correlation between predictors" as a closing remark but provides no diagnostic. Slide 713 explicitly warns that Mathematica's NonLinearModelFit reports a different \(R^2\) the course does not endorse. If asked, name these and state out-of-syllabus.

question: Card 23 — One-way ANOVA F-test

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Model. \(k\) populations, \(n_1,\dots,n_k\), total \(N=\sum n_i\). \(Y_{ij}\sim N(\mu_i,\sigma^2)\) (equal variance across populations). SS decomposition [slides 787–788] : \[ \mathrm{SST} = \mathrm{SSTr} + \mathrm{SSE} \] \[ \mathrm{SSTr}=\sum_i n_i(\bar Y_{i\cdot}-\bar Y_{\cdot\cdot})^2,\;\;\mathrm{SSE}=\sum_i\sum_j(Y_{ij}-\bar Y_{i\cdot})^2 \] Computational form: \(\mathrm{SST}=\sum Y_{ij}^2 - T_{\cdot\cdot}^2/N\); \(\mathrm{SSTr}=\sum_i T_{i\cdot}^2/n_i - T_{\cdot\cdot}^2/N\); \(\mathrm{SSE}=\mathrm{SST}-\mathrm{SSTr}\). Test. \(H_0:\mu_1=\dots=\mu_k\). \(F_{k-1,N-k} = \mathrm{MSTr}/\mathrm{MSE}\) where \(\mathrm{MSTr}=\mathrm{SSTr}/(k-1)\), \(\mathrm{MSE}=\mathrm{SSE}/(N-k)\). Reject if \(F>f_{\alpha,k-1,N-k}\) [slide 797] . Source d.f. SS MS F Treatment \(k-1\) SSTr MSTr MSTr/MSE Error \(N-k\) SSE MSE Total \(N-1\) SST Equal variances are essential [slide 802] ANOVA \(F\) is robust to non-normality at moderate \(n_i\) but not to heteroscedasticity. Bartlett pre-check (Card 24) is the gateway. Worked example (slide 31.6, p. 799–801, coal seams) \(k=5\), \(n_i=7,8,9,8,10\), \(N=42\). SSTr = 3.935 (df 4), SSE = 4.497 (df 37). MSTr = 0.984, MSE = 0.122. \(F=8.095\); p ≈ \(8.6\times 10^{-5}\); reject strongly.

topic_tags: Card 24 — Bartlett's test for equal variances
question: Card 24 — Bartlett's test for equal variances

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: Pre-check for ANOVA. Pooled variance \(\mathrm{MSE}=\sum(n_i-1)S_i^2/(N-k)\): \[ Q = (N-k)\ln\mathrm{MSE} - \sum(n_i-1)\ln S_i^2 \] \[ h = 1 + \frac{1}{3(k-1)}\bigl(\sum_i\frac{1}{n_i-1} - \frac{1}{N-k}\bigr) \] \(B = Q/h \sim \chi^2_{k-1}\) under \(H_0:\sigma_1^2=\dots=\sigma_k^2\). Reject if \(B>\chi^2_{\alpha,k-1}\). Out-of-syllabus Levene's test is not in the slides. If asked to "test for equal variances", use Bartlett.

question: Card 25 — Post-hoc multiple comparisons

solution_steps.label: Recipe
solution_steps.content: The multiplicity problem [slide 814] : with \(m\) tests at level \(\alpha\), experimentwise error \(\leq m\alpha\) (Bonferroni). For \(k=5\) groups, \(m=10\) pairwise tests; experimentwise rate at \(\alpha=0.05\) is \(\approx 40\%\). Fisher LSD (per-comparison) \(|\bar Y_{i\cdot}-\bar Y_{j\cdot}| > t_{\alpha/2,N-k}\sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}(1/n_i+1/n_j)}\) [slide 813] Bonferroni (experimentwise \(\alpha'\)) same as LSD with \(\alpha = \alpha'/[k(k-1)/2]\) [slide 815] Tukey HSD \(|\bar Y_{i\cdot}-\bar Y_{j\cdot}| > r_{k,N-k,\alpha'}\sqrt{(\mathrm{MSE}/2)(1/n_i+1/n_j)}\) [slide 825] Duncan multiple-range test using \(r'_{p,N-k,\alpha'}\); more liberal [slide 831] Out-of-syllabus formulas Scheffé (controls family-wise error across all linear contrasts) and Dunnett (vs. a single control group) are named on slide 829 but formulas are not derived. If asked, cite by name and concept; do not invent formulas. Rule of thumb : pre-specified one or two pairs ⇒ Bonferroni. All \(\binom{k}{2}\) pairs ⇒ Tukey HSD. Decide which comparisons to run before looking at the means. Demo D7 — One-way ANOVA F live (Ch 31) \(\mu_1\) = 10 \(\mu_2\) = 10 \(\mu_3\) = 10 within \(\sigma\) = 2 \(n\) per group = 8 Resample When the three means are equal, \(F\approx 1\) and the observed \(F\) sits in the body of the \(F_{2,3n-3}\). Spread the means apart and watch \(F\) jump into the right tail. Increase \(\sigma\): you need bigger spread to compensate. The shaded red region is the rejection region at \(\alpha=0.05\).

question: T1. Z-test with sample SD

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: problem gives \(s\), no \(\sigma\). Move: T-test.

question: T2. One-sided test compared to \(z_{\alpha/2}\)

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: hypothesis with "\(>\)" or "\(<\)" but user computes \(|z|\). Move: signed \(z\) vs. \(z_\alpha\).

question: T3. "Accept \(H_0\)"

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: same. Move: "fail to reject \(H_0\)".

question: T4. \(\chi^2\)-variance test on non-normal data

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: histogram visibly skewed; no normality justification. Move: flag the issue (slide 444); the course provides no remedy.

question: T5. \(\chi^2\) table read in the wrong tail

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: confusion between "small" and "large" critical. Move: \(\chi^2_{0.95,n-1}<\chi^2_{0.05,n-1}\); left-tail rejection uses the small one.

question: T6. Sign test on the mean

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: prompt says "test that the mean exceeds…" but user uses sign test. Move: sign test is on the median; if symmetric, median = mean.

question: T7. Wilcoxon SR without symmetry

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: distribution clearly skewed (e.g. exponential). Move: sign test instead.

question: T8. Proportion test denom uses \(\hat p\)

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: numbers like "\(0.1\cdot 0.9/n\)" instead of "\(p_0(1-p_0)/n\)". Move: test uses \(p_0\); CI uses \(\hat p\).

question: T9. Pooled-proportion test for \((p_1-p_2)_0\neq 0\)

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: testing \(p_1-p_2=0.05\) but pooling. Move: pooled formula assumes \(p_1=p_2\) (slide 487); use general formula (slide 485).

question: T10. F-test wrong tail / d.f. order

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(F<1\) used in a one-sided right-tail rejection. Move: put larger \(S^2\) on top; or use reciprocal trick (slide 496).

question: T11. Welch d.f. rounded up or kept fractional

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(\nu=11.74\) used as 12. Move: round down to 11 (slide 527).

question: T12. Pre-test variances on same data, then choose pooled vs. Welch

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: workflow "F passes ⇒ pooled T" on same data. Move: invalidates the p-value (slide 521); always use Welch.

question: T13. Treating paired data as two independent samples

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(n_1+n_2-2\) d.f. used when pairs were given. Move: paired T on \(D=X-Y\), d.f. \(=n-1\).

question: T14. Pairing in absence of positive correlation

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: design pairs but variables independent. Move: pairing then loses power (slide 555); use pooled or Welch.

question: T15. Wilcoxon RS used on the larger sample

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(W\) computed from larger sample. Move: use the smaller sample.

question: T16. T-test on \(r\) for \(\rho_0\neq 0\)

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: hypothesis \(\rho=0.5\), formula \(r\sqrt{n-2}/\sqrt{1-r^2}\). Move: T-test only valid for \(\rho_0=0\); use Fisher \(z\).

question: T17. GoF d.f. \(=k-1\) when parameters were estimated

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: Poisson with \(\hat\lambda=\bar X\), but d.f. \(=k-1\). Move: subtract \(m=1\); d.f. \(=k-2\) (slide 577).

question: T18. Cochran violations

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(E_i<5\) for several cells. Move: pool low-\(E\) cells; recompute \(k\) and d.f. (slide 581).

question: T19. \(E_{ij}\) in \(r\times c\) wrong

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(E_{ij}=n_{i\cdot}/r\) or \(n_{\cdot j}/c\). Move: \(E_{ij}=n_{i\cdot}n_{\cdot j}/n\) (slide 595).

question: T20. CI for \(\mu_{Y\mid x^*}\) vs. PI for new \(Y\)

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: same width. Move: PI has "\(1+\)" inside the radical (slide 645).

question: T21. Regression extrapolation

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(x^*\) outside training range. Move: refuse to predict; flag (slide 668).

question: T22. Partial slope as marginal effect on \(Y\)

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: "\(\beta_1=2\) means \(Y\) up by 2 per unit \(x_1\)". Move: "… holding the other predictors fixed ".

question: T23. \(R^2\) chasing

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: model with \(p\to n-1\) predictors. Move: partial-F or PRESS, not raw \(R^2\) (slide 755).

question: T24. Pairwise T-tests without family-wise control

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(\binom{k}{2}\) uncorrected tests post-ANOVA. Move: Bonferroni or Tukey HSD.

question: T25. Tukey HSD with very unequal \(n\)

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: harmonic-mean SE used when \(\max n_i/\min n_i \gg 1\). Move: prefer Bonferroni.

question: T26. ANOVA without checking equal variances

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: jump straight to F-test. Move: Bartlett pre-check (Card 24).

question: T27. Ignoring residual diagnostics

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(R^2\) alone reported. Move: residual plots (slide 668); recall Anscombe quartet.

question: T28. Wrong d.f. in \(S^2\) for MLR

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(S^2=\mathrm{SSE}/(n-2)\) for \(p\geq 2\) model. Move: \(S^2=\mathrm{SSE}/(n-p-1)\) (slide 709).

question: T29. Two-tailed p doubled for already-two-tailed table

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: \(P=2\times 2(1-\Phi(|z|))\). Move: only one factor of 2 (slide 381).

question: T30. Partial F on a single coefficient: forgetting equivalence

solution_steps.label: Diagnostic+Remedy
solution_steps.content: Sign: derived partial F disagrees with \(T^2\). Move: \(T^2 =\) partial F for a single predictor (slide 740); a discrepancy = arithmetic error.

question: A normal sample with \(\sigma=4\) known, \(n=25\), \(\bar x=498.6\). Build the 95% CI for \(\mu\) and use the duality to perform the two-sided test \(H_0:\mu=500\) at \(\alpha=0.05\).

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content: SE \(=\sigma/\sqrt n=4/5=0.8\). \(z_{0.025}=1.96\). CI \(=498.6\pm 1.96\cdot 0.8=[497.03,\,500.17]\). 500 is inside, so by duality fail to reject.

trail_of_thought: 500 sits just inside the upper endpoint — borderline. A one-sided test at \(\alpha=0.05\) would have \(z_{0.05}=1.645\) and CI \([498.6-1.645\cdot 0.8,\,\infty)=[497.28,\infty)\); still contains 500. So even one-sided we wouldn't reject.

question: Two-sided Z-test of \(H_0:\mu=40\), \(\sigma=2\), \(\alpha=0.05\), required power \(0.90\) at \(|\mu-40|\geq 1\). Find minimum \(n\).

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content: \(n\geq ((z_{0.025}+z_{0.10})\sigma/\delta)^2 = ((1.96+1.282)\cdot 2/1)^2 = 6.484^2 \approx 42.04\). Round up to \(n=43\) (or 42 if you take the inequality loosely; the slide example on slide 409 gives 42).

rubric.point: Identify formula (correct one-sided vs two-sided, \(z_{\alpha/2}\) for two-sided).

trail_of_thought: The most common slip: using \(z_{0.05}=1.645\) (one-sided) when the alternative is two-sided. Cross-check: power 0.90 ⇒ \(\beta=0.10\) ⇒ \(z_\beta=z_{0.10}=1.282\), not \(z_{0.05}\). Sanity: doubling \(\delta\) should quarter \(n\); halving \(\delta\) should quadruple it.

question: True or false (justify): "A small p-value means \(H_0\) is unlikely to be true."

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content: False. The p-value is \(P[\text{data at least as extreme}\mid H_0]\), not \(P[H_0\mid \text{data}]\) [slide 385] . Conflating the two is the prosecutor's fallacy. The correct statement: a small p-value means the observed data would be unusual under \(H_0\).

rubric.point: Identify the conditioning direction (\(D\mid H_0\) vs \(H_0\mid D\)).
Mention that going from p-value to \(P[H_0\mid D]\) requires a prior — Bayesian, not in syllabus.

question: Polymer viscosity (normal). \(n=20\), \(s^2=0.0089\). Test \(H_0:\sigma^2=0.005\) vs. two-sided alt., \(\alpha=0.05\).
12 precision washers, \(\bar x=4.985\) mm, \(s=0.027\) mm, normal. Test \(H_0:\mu=5.000\), two-sided, \(\alpha=0.05\).
trail_of_thought: The two off-by-one traps here are d.f. (11 vs 12) and the table column (\(\alpha/2=0.025\) for two-sided, not 0.05). Each flips the answer if you slip.

question: Survey: 985 of 2079 favour a policy. 99% CI for the true proportion, and test \(H_0:p=0.5\) at \(\alpha=0.01\).

rubric.point: CI denom uses \(\hat p\); test denom uses \(p_0\) — explicitly noted.

Notice that 0.5 is just outside the 99% CI yet the two-sided 1% test fails to reject — this means the CI's coverage is slightly miscalibrated relative to the test's 1% level (the duality is approximate for the unpooled-variance proportion case).

question: Two production lines, both normal. Line A: \(n_1=12,\bar x_1=24.6,s_1=0.85\); Line B: \(n_2=15,\bar x_2=22.1,s_2=0.98\). Test means differ at \(\alpha=0.05\) using Welch .
Hardness: 10 specimens each tested with two steel balls. Differences \(d_i = x_i - y_i\) give \(\bar d=8.6\), \(s_D^2=124.7\). Test \(H_0:\mu_D=0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\).

rubric.point: Recognise pairing (10 specimens, both balls each).
Statistic on \(\bar D\), not \(\bar X-\bar Y\).
Plain-English: ball x gives larger hardness measure on average.

trail_of_thought: The trap: treating this as two independent samples, computing pooled or Welch with d.f.=18 or 16; that wastes pairing. Pairing here would be powerful if \(\rho_{XY}>0\) (same specimen gives correlated readings) — and indeed pooled \(t\)≈1.96 fails to reject (slide 23.4 path on slide 5 of the sample-final solution), while paired rejects.

question: Site A: 144/180 recover; Site B: 165/220 recover. Test \(H_0:p_1=p_2\) and report 95% CI for \(p_1-p_2\).

rubric.point: Pooled \(\hat p\) for the test denominator.
Comment on the denominator distinction explicitly.

question: Ch 19, Z-test, σ known A bottling line has \(\sigma=2.0\) mL known, target \(\mu_0=25\) mL. \(n=25\), \(\bar x=24.3\) mL. Test two-sided at \(\alpha=0.05\). Decision and p-value.
trail_of_thought: "Known \(\sigma\)" locks in Z. "differs from" is two-sided ⇒ \(z_{\alpha/2}\). \(\bar x\) below \(\mu_0\) by less than the boundary distance \(0.4\cdot 1.96=0.78\) ⇒ unsurprising fail-to-reject.

question: Ch 19, χ²-variance Polymer viscosity (normal). \(n=20\), \(s^2=0.0089\). Test \(H_0:\sigma^2=0.005\) two-sided at \(\alpha=0.05\). Quote rejection region and decision.

Ch 20, Sign + Wilcoxon SR Eleven workers measured pulse on procedures 1 (X) and 2 (Y). Differences \(Y-X\) = \(17,13,25,12,16,21,14,9,15,6,-1\). Test \(H_0:M_Y\leq M_X\) at \(\alpha=0.05\) using Wilcoxon SR.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content: Only one negative difference (rank 1). \(W^+ = 2+3+\dots+11 = 65\), \(|W^-|=1\). \(W=\min(W^+,|W^-|)=1\). For one-sided \(\alpha=0.05\), \(n=11\): table critical \(=14\); \(1<14\) ⇒ reject.

rubric.point: Quote table critical or normal-approx z (1)

trail_of_thought: From the sample-final solution. Only one negative ⇒ \(|W^-|\) is tiny. The check \(W^+ + |W^-| = n(n+1)/2 = 66\) confirms arithmetic. The decision is one-sided so use the one-sided critical, not \(\alpha/2\).

question: Ch 21, 2-sample proportion 144/180 recover at site A; 165/220 at site B. Test \(p_1=p_2\) and report 95% CI for \(p_1-p_2\).
trail_of_thought: The famous denominator-distinction trap. If you used unpooled in the test you'd get \(z=1.121\) (very close in this case but generally different). Always state which formula you used and why.

question: Ch 25, GoF Defect counts on 60 boards: 32 with 0, 15 with 1, 9 with 2, 4 with \(\geq 3\). Test Poisson at \(\alpha=0.05\).
trail_of_thought: The famous d.f. trap: a student who forgets to subtract \(m=1\) would use \(\nu=2\), \(\chi^2_{0.05,2}=5.99\); same decision here by luck, but on borderline data the wrong d.f. flips the answer.

question: Ch 27, CI vs PI Same SLR; \(\bar x=52.516\). At \(x^*=50\): (a) 95% CI for \(\mu_{Y\mid 50}\); (b) 95% PI for new \(Y\).
Ch 29, partial F Quadratic vs linear, \(n=7\). \(\mathrm{SSE}_\text{full}=1.219\), \(\mathrm{SSE}_\text{red}=2.531\). Partial F at \(\alpha=0.05\).

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content: \(p=2,m=1\); \(F_{1,4}=(4/1)\cdot(2.531-1.219)/1.219=4.31\); \(f_{0.05,1,4}=7.71\); fail to reject. The quadratic term doesn't add significantly over the linear.

question: Ch 31, ANOVA F Coal seams, \(k=5\), \(N=42\). SSTr = 3.935, SSE = 4.497. Build the ANOVA table and conclude at \(\alpha=0.05\).

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content: df: 4 / 37 / 41. MS: 0.984 / 0.122. F = 8.095. \(f_{0.05,4,37}\approx 2.61\); reject \(H_0\) strongly. Some seam means differ; next step is Tukey HSD or Bonferroni.

question: Ch 32, Tukey HSD Same coal data: \(\mathrm{MSE}=0.1215\), \(N-k=37\). Means \(\bar y=(1.66, 1.17, 1.46, 0.88, 0.88)\), sizes \((7,8,9,8,10)\). At experiment-wise \(\alpha'=0.01\) (\(r_{5,37,0.01}=4.96\)), which seams differ?

rubric.point: State implicit underlining of non-different groups (1)

question: Cross-cut, sample size Two-sided Z-test of \(H_0:\mu=40\), \(\sigma=2\), \(\alpha=0.05\), required power 0.90 at \(|\mu-40|\geq 1\). Find min \(n\).

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content: \(n\geq((z_{0.025}+z_{0.10})\sigma/\delta)^2 = ((1.96+1.282)\cdot 2/1)^2 = 6.484^2 = 42.04\). Round up to \(n=43\) (slide 409 reports 42 with rounding flexibility — accept either 42 or 43).

trail_of_thought: The most common slip is \(z_{0.05}=1.645\) instead of \(z_{0.025}\) for a two-sided alternative. If you halve the detectable \(\delta\) you should quadruple \(n\); if you go to one-sided \(\alpha\), \(n\) drops by \(\approx 17\%\) for the same power.

question: Correlation A bivariate normal sample of \(n=20\) gives \(r=0.80\). Test \(H_0:\rho=0.5\) at \(\alpha=0.05\), and report a 95% CI for \(\rho\).
trail_of_thought: The trap: writing \(T=r\sqrt{n-2}/\sqrt{1-r^2}=0.80\sqrt{18}/\sqrt{0.36}=5.66\) — but that null distribution is only valid at \(\rho_0=0\). Using it would massively overstate evidence here.

question: F-test variances SC: \(n_1=10\), \(s_1^2=0.024\). ME: \(n_2=12\), \(s_2^2=0.181\). Test \(H_0:\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2\) at \(\alpha=0.05\) two-sided.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content: \(F=s_2^2/s_1^2 = 0.181/0.024 = 7.54\) (put larger on top). d.f. (11,9). Two-sided ⇒ compare to \(f_{0.025,11,9}\). From standard tables ≈ 3.91. \(7.54>3.91\); reject. Evidence variances differ.

rubric.point: Right d.f. order (\(n_2-1, n_1-1\) for the ratio orientation) (1)

trail_of_thought: If the slide has only the upper-tail F table, you might need the reciprocal trick: \(f_{0.025,11,9}\) is what you want; if your table only goes up to d.f. 10 in the numerator, swap to \(f_{0.025,9,11}\) and reciprocate. Cross-checks: variances genuinely differ by a factor of 7+; don't expect to fail to reject.

question: Sample size, proportion How many subjects to estimate \(p\) within \(\pm 0.02\) at 90% confidence, with no prior estimate?
Lack-of-fit Chemical yield, \(k=5\) distinct \(x\)'s, three replicates each (\(n=15\)). \(\mathrm{SSE}=58.58\); \(\mathrm{SSE}_\mathrm{pe}=6.45\). Test linear adequacy at \(\alpha=0.05\).

rubric.point: Decision: linear inadequate; consider quadratic or transformation (1)

trail_of_thought: The slope T-test on this same data is highly significant — but slope significance does NOT imply correct shape. A curved-but-positive trend will reject \(\beta_1=0\) easily even when the linear model is wrong. Lack-of-fit catches this; nothing else does.
